All right guys, well welcome back to the final exercise in module two and I'm gonna warn you right from the beginning, this is the longest one, there are 47 questions here and I go through all of these before I even think about writing an email. Before I even approach a funnel, these are really the questions that fuel it, that really steep me in who I'm talking to and what drives them and what motivates them and where we're taking them. So these are all essential things I need to know if I'm gonna be able to coach that conversion, if I'm gonna be able to even know what that transformation is between point A and point buyer. So this is gonna be a bit of a longer walkthrough but here's the caveat, you don't have to do it all in one sitting. When I choose to do this all in one sitting, it's about a 60 minute to two hour exercise depending on how awake I am or what point of the day it is but another way to approach this is these questions will reappear between modules three and seven, particularly for the point of the funnel, the subsequence we're looking at where these questions become more relevant to the content you have to develop and the aspects of the conversion that you're coaching. So if you don't do them all now, they will appear again later. I highly recommend it if you can wing it to steep in them right now and looking at them again later will actually just reinforce and maybe even reveal more insights. But here's one thing I do wanna say about this, this is really the pinnacle of what I call direct response 2.0 is just asking these really deep questions that help you dive deeper into not just your prospect but you yourself, your business, the heart of it, the pulse of it and you know just like how journaling is good for the soul answering these questions just unlock so many insights and data and information about you and what you're doing and it is not uncommon whatsoever for these questions to go beyond the scope of just writing copy but actually driving new product ideas, new aspects of your business, new things you want to do within it or new ways you want to serve. So it is that powerful. I really hope that it unlocks all those things for you but what we're really getting at right now is just unlocking the information and the insights and the understandings that are gonna help you be super steeped in the process and then even more so when you revisit them later. So the first section I wanna look at is your relationship to your avatar. So simply what, why do you do, what do you do? And here's the biggie, you know what about your market's struggles breaks your heart. There was a time not too long ago when I was going through a period of stagnation and I was speaking with one of my mentors at the time. His name is David Wagner. He's just this awesome, awesome coach and yoga teacher and just does so many awesome things, awesome work and he was coaching me and I told him, you know, like I just don't really have any intense desires or longings, you know, I'm feeling complacent but I'm not feeling like that's a problem, you know, like where else is there to go from here? You know, I'm cool, I'm fine and then he instantly broke through that with one question. He just stopped me. He said, I hear you, Ryan, but tell me, what breaks your heart more than anything? You know, and this just pulled me out from that mentation, this rationalization that I was okay and from there the bloodletting began. So you know, Hemingway once said writing is easy, all you have to do is puncture a vein and bleed onto the page and a hundred years later what we're doing here is puncturing your heart and seeing what bleeds out onto the pixels and that is super powerful. I want you to stick with that. What about your market struggles breaks your heart and what reality do you see for them that they don't yet see for themselves and what pains you about their inability to see that potential? What was your biggest failure as a coach educator program creator? How do you feel you may have let your market down and what did you learn from that experience? What vows did you make from that moment? And this is just extremely grounding to show that you're not this perfect Guru on a mountain, you know, but that you have your goals, you know, you have your goals and how you're able to serve, you know, and when you can communicate that, why you do what you do, how you failed before you get them on your side to accomplish what you're trying to achieve. So that kind of makes the buying decision less just about them and also it allows them to be empathetic and serve you through their purchase decision in a weird kind of cool way. So that's a big one. What's the most gratifying or fulfilling experience you've had as a coach educator or program creator and how did that experience exceed your expectations and maybe even open up your eyes to a deeper impact that you didn't even know you were making? And to thank you, welcome video after you enrolled. You saw the story of how a few years ago someone once said, Ryan, we're not saving lives. And that really, really pissed me off because I take this seriously. And I knew there was a reason I take this seriously. And it wasn't until a few months ago when one of my clients sent me an email with the subject line that said, your copy caused this. And it was just this amazing story of a woman who was not going to be in the program, but then she joined based on one of the final emails. And now she's achieving literally life changing results and getting that mentorship and guidance and education she needed. So that totally blew my expectations of writing copy. So what about, what is it about what you do? Or what is that experience that has exceeded your expectations? That could be a really big moment to connect with your audience on. And finally, like how deep is your commitment to serving this market? How have you re-engineered your own life to create that impact? What have you sacrificed or given up in the name of that mission? I write copy because I care about my ripple of impact more than anything. And there's only so much I could do under the name, Rye Schwartz. But when I lend these words, this copy, this marketing to all these amazing authors who are in a place to do so much good in their own way, that is how my ripple of impact expands beyond a reach that I can ever see. So yeah, talk about that in your marketing as well. It's a connection point. And what have you learned from your market? How have they inspired you? Once again, this is very grounding and shows humility. And finally, the last one for this section at least is the world will be a better place when. And that really kind of keys you in. Once again, it's another approach to why you do what you do. Moving on, we have your avatars perceived are real limitations. And we're going to get into this deeper later. So I'm just going to kind of go quicker here. What's the biggest excuse your avatar makes for having not achieved a favorable outcome yet? And how might that excuse be justified? And maybe how can you even validate that excuse and say, you're right, it wasn't your fault. This deck was stacked against you. And just really confirm that. But the important thing is to follow that up with what is it about you or your method that overcomes that limitation? So if you're going to validate it, that's powerful. But you need to create this dynamic of it's not your fault until it is because now you can overcome that. And how do your biggest competitors fall short in overcoming it? That's another big one as far as positioning goes. What is a false belief they have about the industry? What's a false belief they have about themselves and their abilities or capacities to achieve it? What about the best way to solve the problem or achieve the outcome? What false beliefs do they have around that? And number eight is a really big one for me. I've used this for a lot of the big launches I've done, but why might it be a good thing they've waited until now? So especially if you're teaching a topic that isn't particularly new, you're a little late to the game in teaching it. You kind of want to show why it's a good thing they've waited until now too. You've waited a while to come out with your course, but why is it a good thing they've waited until now to get in? When I was helping Amy Porterfield sell her courses, courses that convert course and her webinars that convert course, there were already tons of courses on those topics. But we knew that our market either wasn't getting those results yet or they haven't even attempted it. So we had to communicate why that might have been a good thing. We tend to feel a lot of guilt and shame around our procrastination because a lot of people procrastinate to shame us. We procrastinate to shame ourselves. So that's a really amazing bonding experience when you can prove and show in a believable and sincere way why it's a good thing they've waited. And then what are maybe the five biggest objections they might have around forging ahead with your product? Each can you explore how you can create more comfort and ease around it? And then here's a big one we're going to explore deeper. We already touched upon it a bit in a previous exercise, but what are those sacred cows with sour milk? So those commonly held and widely accepted beliefs and paradigms that don't serve your prospects. So the ones they're most ready and willing to be released from. And then we have what I call ethos graphics. This goes beyond just demographics and psychographics. This really goes down to what they believe, what they feel, how they see themselves in this world. So what are they tired of seeing? What makes them sick? So is that marketing gurus with hyped up promises and rented lambos and all that scarcity and urgency tactics and 48 hours and 24 hours and your life is going to be terrible if you don't buy an app? That's what makes our market really sick. And you guys want to be able to sell more, but you don't want to be able to just kind of lather on that FOMO that makes everyone feel queasy. What is it about the way you run your business or program that will resonate with their personal values? And one thing that is brilliant is what Rameet Sethi does, or at least he used to do it, where he'd urged people not to incur credit card debt to enroll in his programs. And this really shows that preeminence that he's looking out for his audience, that he doesn't want people to put themselves in vulnerable positions to work with him. So it sets that standard for his audience, but it also sets that standard for himself and people respect people who maintain these honorable values and qualities. So what is it about the way you run your business or program that will resonate with your audience's personal values? How does your tribe inspire you to be better? Simple but powerful, go there and connect with them over those aspects of what you discover. And what do you refuse to let them be exposed to or injure anymore? You know, I've said it before, but FOMO is something you should be feeling when you feel their opportunity to escape exposure to that thing is slipping. So what is that thing you refuse to let them expose to or injure anymore? For me and in this course, I refuse to let skilled coaches and educators have that funnel anxiety or worse start building funnels and product launches, but never get them off the ground because they realize there's this missing ingredient called email copy. So that's what I refuse to let our audience and you guys be exposed to anymore. But what is that in your business? And in what other areas can you take a stand with them or for them? You know, think of where competitors or common conventions have let them down and left them feeling disillusioned. You know, you want to break through that disillusionment and give them hope again. Moving on, these are some other powerful questions that have unlocked some pretty cool things. I know that's a recurring theme here, but that's why they're here. You know, that's why there are 47 of them because these are the 47 I go to and maybe in a few months there will be 57. For now, these are really the ones that I actually return to in every launch. Exclusive empowerment and self forgiveness. What skills, characteristics, experiences, circumstances or points of pride exclusively empower your prospect to achieve the end result. And what missteps or regrets or sunk costs might they need to forgive themselves for before being willing to change. And this really plays into a lot of cognitive bias. You know, sometimes making a decision to buy your program would mean admitting fault, at least on a subtle unconscious level, that they messed up and that could be painful. So you want to actually facilitate that forgiveness. And there's a theme plate for that market search for meaning that you'll see a little later if you have six figure emails. And how many of those previous failures can be reframed as necessary steps or prerequisites for where they are now. So that's where it comes full circle. You close that meaning loop, you create meaning around those previous mess ups or regrets and you make it okay. You make it part of that journey, part of that vindication. And then we have what I call neck brace moments. And these are where you get your prospect nodding their head with excitement and just kind of saying, wow, someone finally gets me. Because it's just super gratifying and freeing to have our experience but verified and validated to us. So these are the four questions that I go there. What can you confirm to them about their present experience struggle as it relates to the problem your product solves? Just confirm their reality, confirm what they're seeing. Can you confirm to them why solving this really matters in their life? So if you can reflect back their intention deeper than they're able to recognize it themselves or deeper than they're able to articulate it themselves, but they can still see that when it's reflected back to them. That is a very powerful moment that builds trust and affinity. And what can you confirm to them about their skepticism and reasons for being rightfully skeptical? Once again, validation. That's where the buyer's journey starts is in just validating these pieces of their current context back to them. And where have they been most let down or disillusion? Because this is the thing that's going to give them the most resistance around going on this journey. Like, oh, no, not again. I've seen this before. So how have they been most let down or disillusion? And how can you really cut through that? And then we get into your product. This extends more into offer creation. And if you've taken a course on building your course or program chances, or you've seen some of these, but in case not, I do want to at least give you a chance to dive into them. So what is that magic wand moment or outcome your product can deliver? And why is this important to your prospect? Or why is it what they really need? You know, and on an even deeper level, why does that matter? And then on another even deeper level, why does that matter? And I'm stumbling over my words because my mind is racing as I go down those levels. But I want you to do the same. I want you to go down those rabbit holes because that's where these discoveries are found. And what new opportunities will arise as a result? You know, and how do we frame this as a push goal that opens them up to new favorable opportunities? Can we connect this outcome to other important wants? You know, for example, if you're teaching someone how to build an email list, and that's your program, think of all the next outcomes they can now even pursue. You know, they can now create a product, sell to it. They can do affiliate promotions. They could, it just opens them up to all these possibilities that they've wanted to capitalize on the past, but regretfully haven't been able to do so. So how can you frame the outcome of your course as a push goal for other favorable opportunities? And what other intangible or unexpected problems does buying your product solve? You know, one thing I like to touch upon a lot is free freeing them from the search. You know, how much energy do we spend in comparing solutions and, you know, just being on the sidelines and watching all these launches or opportunities go by and that consumes a lot of energy. So freeing someone from a search is a very powerful but intangible outcome. You know, and there's confidence to double down, you know, knowing that they're getting that return on effort. This is so universal across everything we're selling. People aren't afraid of spending time. They're afraid of wasting time. So giving someone a return on effort, making them feel confident enough to double down on a solution is a very powerful unexpected outcome that a product can deliver. Then we get into the four levels of benefits they'll experience using your product. And I'll let you go through these yourself, but essentially we could have the tangibles and we could have the intangibles and we have some that are immediate and some that are long term and just filling these all out will give you kind of a more holistic, broadened range of what your product actually provides and more importantly give you the chance to reflect those back to your prospect. And finally, this is a very powerful future pacing exercise, you know, that I call the parallel realities divergent roads exercise essentially, but in vivid detail, what does success in your program look like three days from now? So this is something they could still easily visualize, you know, they're probably even wearing the same socks if they don't do laundry very often. What does it look like in three weeks, three months, 12 months? So it gets harder and harder, you know, the future gets a blurrier and blurrier, but if you can reflect that reality gradually, you know, from three days, three weeks, then you earn that kind of believability and credibility that you could know what it looks like in three months and 12 months, especially if you've done your job and accurately reflected back the earlier ones. And then in vivid detail to contrast that, what would their life look like three days from now if they don't buy a product three weeks, three months, 12 months. And obviously we're doing this with an, with an agenda of proving to them beyond doubt that our product will offer them the more favorable alternate reality or parallel reality, but you don't want to lose credibility by exaggerating just how good, you know, be really honest and sincere of what you think the reality will look like under both, both potential outcomes. So this is kind of like that butterfly effect, you know, one with your product and one without it. So with that said, we just did a full overview of the 47 questions. If you're doing it right now, awesome, amazing. If not, you're going to certainly see them a little later. So at least now, hopefully you've steeped in them a bit. You've gotten an idea of what we're trying to do with all of them and feel free to rewind back to this video in case you want to just kind of give it a second look while you're doing it later. So that's it for now. And with that said, we are now ready to dive into module three.
